title: Structures

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Structures; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ISO C Working Group C23 status; ISO/IEC 9899:2024; provisions applicable to Structures
sources.claim: A structure groups ordered named members in one object, may contain padding for alignment, and supports whole-structure assignment and value passing when types match.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A structure groups ordered named members in one object, may contain padding for alignment, and supports whole-structure assignment and value passing when types match.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A struct Point stores named x and y members, is initialized with designated initializers, and is copied by structure assignment. Your goal is to model and process a small domain entity using a structure and functions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: One-dimensional arrays; Addresses and dereference. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A structure groups ordered named members in one object, may contain padding for alignment, and supports whole-structure assignment and value passing when types match. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Padding bytes can be indeterminate, member layout has implementation constraints, flexible array members need special allocation, and bytewise comparison is not field equality. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: memcmp is always a correct equality test for structures because all members occupy one contiguous object.

includes: Define, initialize, copy and access related named fields.
Model and process a small domain entity using a structure and functions.
excludes: Padding bytes can be indeterminate, member layout has implementation constraints, flexible array members need special allocation, and bytewise comparison is not field equality.

outcomes: Model and process a small domain entity using a structure and functions.

examples.title: Structures: defined case
examples.situation: A struct Point stores named x and y members, is initialized with designated initializers, and is copied by structure assignment.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A struct Point stores named x and y members, is initialized with designated initializers, and is copied by structure assignment.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Compare structure values by their meaningful members; padding can differ or be indeterminate even when every member value is equal.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Compare structure values by their meaningful members; padding can differ or be indeterminate even when every member value is equal.

examples.title: Structures: boundary case
examples.situation: Two logically equal structures are compared member by member rather than with memcmp because padding bytes need not match.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Two logically equal structures are compared member by member rather than with memcmp because padding bytes need not match.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Padding bytes can be indeterminate, member layout has implementation constraints, flexible array members need special allocation, and bytewise comparison is not field equality.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: memcmp is always a correct equality test for structures because all members occupy one contiguous object.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Model and process a small domain entity using a structure and functions.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: memcmp is always a correct equality test for structures because all members occupy one contiguous object.
misconceptions.correction: Compare structure values by their meaningful members; padding can differ or be indeterminate even when every member value is equal.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: memcmp is always a correct equality test for structures because all members occupy one contiguous object.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Structures: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Padding bytes can be indeterminate, member layout has implementation constraints, flexible array members need special allocation, and bytewise comparison is not field equality.
